meta.notes: Dimensions 1-3 of 8 (5 free + 3 paid). Maps to quotients VP, AS, CC. Conversational tone, culturally resonant scenarios. Designed for 25-45 year old professionals.

dimensions.title: Values & Priorities
dimensions.tagline: The hot takes that reveal what actually matters to you.
dimensions.description: Measures alignment on life direction, moral flexibility, and how central the relationship is in your world. Values misalignment is the single strongest predictor of long-term relationship failure -- couples can negotiate habits but not core beliefs.
dimensions.scientific_basis: Adapted from the Schwartz Values Survey (SVS) 10 universal value domains, filtered to the 4 domains most predictive of romantic satisfaction. Supplemented with Relationship Priority items from Aron & Aron's Inclusion of Other in the Self scale. Schwartz (1992) demonstrated cross-cultural stability across 82 countries; Leikas et al. (2018) confirmed value similarity predicts relationship satisfaction beyond personality.
dimensions.compatibility_rationale: Value similarity -- not complementarity -- predicts long-term relationship satisfaction. Partners do not benefit from opposing values; they benefit from shared ones.

sub_scales.name: Life Direction
sub_scales.description: Where you want your life to go -- career ambition vs. family focus vs. adventure-seeking vs. stability-building.

sub_scales.name: Moral Flexibility
sub_scales.description: How you process gray areas -- moral absolutism vs. contextual ethics. Not about right or wrong, but about how you navigate ambiguity.

sub_scales.name: Relationship Priority
sub_scales.description: How central the romantic relationship is in your life hierarchy.

questions.text: Your partner gets a dream job offer across the country. You just got promoted. What happens?
questions.scoring_note: High = relationship-centric life direction. Low = career/independence priority.

questions.text: You just inherited $500K. No strings attached. What's your first move?
questions.scoring_note: Measures life priority orientation: security (1-2), balance (3), experience (4), family (5).

questions.text: Curb Your Enthusiasm or Martin?
questions.scoring_note: Cultural alignment proxy. Measures humor style and cultural touchstones. Used for profile matching, not judgment.

questions.text: Should Barry Bonds be in the Hall of Fame even though he used steroids at the end of his career?
questions.scoring_note: Low = moral absolutism (rules are rules). High = contextual ethics (nuance matters). Neither is better; used for similarity matching.

questions.text: Your best friend tells you they cheated on their partner. They're crying. What do you do?
questions.scoring_note: Low = accountability-first orientation. High = compassion-first orientation. Measures moral processing style.

questions.text: How someone treats a waiter tells you everything you need to know about them.
questions.scoring_note: High = strong moral conviction applied broadly. Low = contextual/flexible judgment.

questions.text: Your career is on fire right now. Your partner says they feel neglected. Honestly, what do you feel first?
questions.scoring_note: Low = career-first instinct. High = relationship-first instinct. Measures gut-level priority hierarchy.

questions.text: Real talk: a great relationship is something you fit into a great life, or something you build your life around?
questions.scoring_note: Low = relationship as component. High = relationship as foundation.

scoring.method: Sub-scale means computed separately via scenario response values (1-5). Combined as weighted average.
scoring.compatibility_function: Similarity: 1 - abs(partner_A - partner_B) / 4, normalized to 0-1. Multiply by weight (18) for CIS contribution.

dimensions.title: Attachment Style
dimensions.tagline: How you love when nobody's watching.
dimensions.description: Maps your attachment pattern -- how you seek closeness, handle separation, and respond to perceived threats in a relationship.
dimensions.scientific_basis: Adapted from the ECR-R (Experiences in Close Relationships - Revised, Fraley et al. 2000). The ECR-R is the gold-standard self-report measure of adult attachment. Items reworded as culturally resonant scenarios while preserving the two-dimensional structure (anxiety x avoidance) plus a Security sub-scale.
dimensions.compatibility_rationale: Secure + Any style = best outcomes. Anxious + Avoidant = worst outcomes (pursue-withdraw cycle). Scoring applies BONUS when either partner is secure, PENALTY for anxious-avoidant pairings.

sub_scales.name: Attachment Anxiety
sub_scales.description: Fear of abandonment, need for reassurance, hypervigilance to relationship threats.

sub_scales.name: Attachment Avoidance
sub_scales.description: Discomfort with emotional closeness, compulsive self-reliance, tendency to withdraw under stress.

sub_scales.name: Attachment Security
sub_scales.description: Baseline felt safety in relationships, comfort with both intimacy and autonomy.

questions.text: Your partner hasn't texted back in 6 hours. You...
questions.scoring_note: High = attachment anxiety. Hypervigilance to availability signals.

questions.text: How many times have you gone back to an ex? Be honest.
questions.scoring_note: High = attachment patterns indicating difficulty letting go. Anxious preoccupation marker.

questions.text: After an argument, you can't sleep until things are resolved. Even if it's 2 AM.
questions.scoring_note: High = difficulty self-soothing, need for immediate repair. Core anxious attachment behavior.

questions.text: Your partner wants to combine finances after 6 months. Your gut says...
questions.scoring_note: High = avoidant (resistance to enmeshment). Low = comfort with interdependence.

questions.text: When things get emotionally intense in a relationship, your instinct is to...
questions.scoring_note: High = avoidant deactivation under stress. Low = secure/anxious approach behavior.

questions.text: An ex once described you as 'hard to read.' Your honest reaction?
questions.scoring_note: High = self-identified emotional guardedness. Low = self-identified openness.

questions.text: Your partner is going through it. Like, really going through it. You...
questions.scoring_note: High = secure caregiving. Ability to show up without enmeshment or withdrawal.

questions.text: Can you tell your partner what you actually need without worrying they'll think you're 'too much'?
questions.scoring_note: High = felt security in expressing needs. Low = insecure self-silencing.

scoring.method: Sub-scale means from scenario responses. Classification derived from anxiety/avoidance/security profile.

compatibility_function.secure_bonus: +0.20 if either partner classifies as secure
compatibility_function.both_secure_bonus: +0.30 if BOTH partners classify as secure
compatibility_function.anxious_avoidant_penalty: -0.35 if one is high-anxious AND the other is high-avoidant
compatibility_function.final_range: Clamp to [0, 1]. Multiply by weight (20) for CIS contribution.

dimensions.title: Communication & Conflict
dimensions.tagline: How you fight matters more than how you flirt.
dimensions.description: How you fight, how you make up, and how openly you share what's actually going on inside. Communication style is the most trainable dimension but the most immediately damaging when mismatched.
dimensions.scientific_basis: Integrates Gottman's Four Horsemen framework (predicts divorce with 93.6% accuracy) and the Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Mode Instrument (TKI). Repair attempts are weighted highest as Gottman's #1 predictor of relationship success.
dimensions.compatibility_rationale: It's not whether couples fight but HOW they fight and whether they can repair. Repair capacity transcends conflict style.

sub_scales.name: Conflict Style
sub_scales.description: Your default mode when tension arises -- lean in, shut down, accommodate, or negotiate.

sub_scales.name: Repair Capacity
sub_scales.description: Ability and willingness to de-escalate, take accountability, and reconnect after conflict.

sub_scales.name: Emotional Expression
sub_scales.description: How openly and directly you share your internal emotional state.

questions.text: Your partner roasts you a little too hard in front of friends. You...
questions.scoring_note: Measures conflict approach from avoidant (1) to confrontational (5).

questions.text: During a heated argument, I have a hard time stopping myself from saying things I know will hurt.
questions.scoring_note: High = contempt/hostility under stress (Gottman Horsemen #1 and #2). Red flag item at score 5.

questions.text: Your partner says 'we need to talk.' Your heart rate goes from 0 to...
questions.scoring_note: Measures conflict-related anxiety. High = hyperactivation to conflict cues.

questions.text: How long can you hold a grudge? Be honest.
questions.scoring_note: High = strong forgiveness capacity. Low = poor repair ability.

questions.text: After a bad fight, who reaches out first to make up?
questions.scoring_note: High = strong repair initiation. Gottman's #1 predictor of relationship success.

questions.text: I can genuinely apologize and mean it, even when I think I was only partly wrong.
questions.scoring_note: High = accountability and partial-fault ownership. Critical repair skill.

questions.text: You've had a terrible day. Your partner asks how you're doing. You say...
questions.scoring_note: Low = emotional suppression. High = emotional transparency.

questions.text: You're feeling insecure about where things stand with your partner. Do you tell them?
questions.scoring_note: High = vulnerability willingness. Combines emotional expression with courage.

scoring.method: Sub-scale means. Repair weighted highest. Complementarity scoring with repair weighting.

compatibility_function.horseman_penalty: If m3_cc_02 >= 4 for either partner, apply -0.10 penalty
compatibility_function.final_range: Clamp to [0, 1]. Multiply by weight (17) for CIS contribution.
